summary: A collection of excerpts from Mary Wollstonecraft's writings, primarily focusing on her arguments for women's education, the natural rights of mothers, the necessity of equality in romantic relationships, and the destructive nature of societal expectations that encourage female folly.

principles.statement: Women must be educated to be companions to men, or they will halt the progress of society.
principles.rationale: Truth and virtue must be accessible to everyone in order to influence general practice; an uneducated woman cannot contribute to the progress of knowledge.
principles.source_quote: Contending for the rights of woman, my main argument is built on this simple principle, that if she be not prepared by education to become the companion of man, she will stop the progress of knowledge and virtue; for truth must be common to all

principles.statement: Emancipation and shared rights are prerequisites for women to develop true virtue.
principles.rationale: When women are freed from arbitrary authority and allowed to share in human rights, they will naturally grow more perfect and emulate the virtues traditionally associated with men.
principles.source_quote: Let woman share the rights and she will emulate the virtues of man; for she must grow more perfect when emancipated, or justify the authority that chains such a weak being to her duty.

principles.statement: Mothers possess a natural right to their children due to the burdens of pregnancy and care.
principles.rationale: Women bear the physical care and anxiety of bringing a child into the world, whereas men often detach themselves from these duties to immerse themselves in the world.
principles.source_quote: Considering the care and anxiety a woman must have about her child before it comes into the world, it seems to me, by a natural right, to belong to her.

principles.statement: The purest form of love requires absolute intellectual equality.
principles.rationale: True love grows through equal advances in the minds of both partners, rejecting the traditional customs that give one sex priority or impose artificial delicacy on the other.
principles.source_quote: The partiality we conceived for each other was in that mode which I have always considered as the purest and most refined style of love. It grew with equal advances in the mind of each... One sex did not take the priority which long-established custom has awarded it

mental_models.name: The Feathered Race Analogy
mental_models.description: A comparative lens using the behavior of birds to highlight the unnaturalness of human male abandonment of parental duties. It contrasts the cooperative parenting of animals with the entitlement of human men who claim children without sharing the burden of care.
mental_models.example: Wollstonecraft notes that 'whilst the hen keeps the young warm, her mate stays by to cheer her; but it is sufficient for man to condescend to get a child, in order to claim it.'

signature_quotes.text: Contending for the rights of woman, my main argument is built on this simple principle, that if she be not prepared by education to become the companion of man, she will stop the progress of knowledge and virtue; for truth must be common to all, or it will be inefficacious with respect to its influence on general practice.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing her core argument for women's rights and education.

signature_quotes.text: Let woman share the rights and she will emulate the virtues of man; for she must grow more perfect when emancipated, or justify the authority that chains such a weak being to her duty.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing that emancipation is necessary for moral perfection.

signature_quotes.text: When men get immersed in the world, they seem to lose all sensations, excepting those necessary to continue or produce life! Are these the privileges of reason?
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing men's abandonment of parental and emotional duties.

signature_quotes.text: I consider fidelity and constancy as two distinct things; yet the former is necessary to give life to the other, and such a degree of respect do I think due to myself, that, if only probity, which is a good thing in its place, brings you back, never return...
signature_quotes.context: Writing to Imlay about the standards of respect and genuine affection she requires in a relationship.

anti_patterns.description: Women cultivating foolishness to appear desirable to men.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It degrades the entire sex and allows foolish women to rob wiser, more sensible women of opportunities for genuine connection, simply because men are entertained by 'pretty folly'.

anti_patterns.description: Maintaining a romantic relationship out of mere duty or 'probity'.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It violates one's self-respect. Fidelity and constancy require genuine passion and life, not just a begrudging sense of moral obligation to return to a partner.
